title: Converting a single-flush toilet to dual flush
subtitle: Replacing a siphon mechanism with a dual-flush valve to halve flushing water use
excerpt: A standard single-flush toilet uses 9-13 litres per flush. A dual-flush toilet uses 6 litres (full flush) or 3 litres (half flush). Replacing the siphon mechanism inside a single-flush cistern with an aftermarket dual-flush valve takes about 30 minutes and requires no plumbing skills beyond turning off the water supply.
sourceNotes: Waterwise UK guidance on toilet water consumption. WRAS (Water Regulations Advisory Scheme) guidance on cistern components. Interflush and Sanakey product installation guides for retrofit dual-flush valves.

glossaryTerms.definition: The traditional single-flush mechanism inside a toilet cistern. When the handle is pressed, a diaphragm creates a siphon effect that empties the whole cistern in one flush. Cannot be interrupted mid-flush. Most UK toilets installed before 2001 use siphon mechanisms.

A replacement cistern mechanism with two flush volumes: a full flush (typically 6 litres) and a reduced flush (typically 3-4 litres). Retrofit valves replace the siphon mechanism in an existing cistern and are compatible with most standard cistern sizes.

content.text: Identifying whether your toilet has a

: remove the cistern lid and press the handle. A siphon lifts a rubber diaphragm and you will see water rushing out from the bottom of a central tube. If it empties completely every time regardless of how briefly you press, it is a siphon. A
replacement (brands: Interflush, Sanakey, Thomas Dudley Turbo88) fits in most standard 9-inch close-coupled cisterns.
Turn off the water supply to the cistern at the isolation valve below the cistern (turn a flat-head screw a quarter turn so the slot is across the pipe, not in line).
Flush to empty the cistern. Use a sponge to remove the remaining water from the bottom.
Unscrew the locking nut at the base of the siphon (turn anticlockwise viewed from below). Lift out the old siphon.
Insert the new dual-flush valve through the base hole. Hand-tighten the locking nut from below; do not overtighten; the rubber washer provides the seal.
Reconnect the flush handle linkage to the new valve's buttons or lever as per the instructions.
Open the isolation valve and allow the cistern to fill. Check for leaks at the base nut. Test both flush volumes.

Some low-profile or concealed cisterns do not accommodate standard retrofit valves. Some older high-level cisterns have a different mechanism. In these cases, the alternative is a cistern displacement device (a bag or bottle filled with water and placed inside the cistern, reducing effective flush volume without mechanism replacement). These save approximately 1-2 litres per flush at no installation cost.
